quote: Just to make sure, you know, we're on the same page. So you were looking more of, like, just the equipment aspects, not like a full installation or anything like that?
stewart_read: The rep's clarifying question confirms James's framing rather than opening a new one. By asking 'just the equipment aspects, not like a full installation?' the rep hands James a clean exit ramp and validates his self-disqualification. Strategic read: the rep is trying to understand the objection before addressing it. Reactive read: the rep is inadvertently co-authoring the customer's reason to leave. The reactive read holds — there is no subsequent move that uses this information to re-engage.
alternative_hypothesis: The rep was gathering information to set up a reframe — a legitimate first step in multi-angle handling.
coaching_implication: The clarifying question is not wrong in isolation, but it needs a follow-up move. In the 1-on-1, ask the rep: 'Once you confirmed what he was looking for, what was your plan to use that?' The answer will reveal whether this was a setup or a dead end. Coach toward using the customer's own framing as the door: James said he was interested in subsidies and panel costs — those are curiosity hooks, not disqualifiers.

quote: I think the the leading ad that I saw showed that there were still subsidies available for solar installations.
stewart_read: James volunteered his original motivation unprompted: he saw an ad about subsidies and wanted to understand the cost. This is a genuine interest signal buried inside a disengagement speech. The rep did not anchor on it. A customer who came in asking about subsidies and cost is not a dead lead — he is a customer whose curiosity was never properly engaged.
coaching_implication: In the 1-on-1, replay this moment and ask the rep: 'He told you exactly why he filled out the form — what could you have done with that?' The coaching move is to anchor on the subsidy interest: 'The subsidies are actually part of what our specialist walks through — they vary by system size and your usage. That's exactly the kind of thing the design appointment covers.' This is curiosity creation, not a pitch.

quote: I really don't need solar panels. My electricity usage is relatively small, and payback period is probably too long.
stewart_read: James has done his own payback math and concluded solar doesn't pencil. This is a knowledge gap masquerading as a settled decision — he is estimating payback without knowing Ion's actual numbers, current incentive structures, or what 'relatively small' usage looks like against a solar offset. The rep did not challenge the math or create curiosity about whether his estimate is accurate. The flip was available: 'Most people who say that are surprised when they see the actual design — the payback period depends on factors you can't calculate without the system design. That's what the appointment is for.'
alternative_hypothesis: James's usage genuinely may be too low to make solar viable — the rep may have correctly read a DQ signal and not wasted time.
coaching_implication: The alternative hypothesis is worth testing in the 1-on-1: ask the rep whether she thought James was a real lead or a DQ. If she thought DQ, coach on the difference between a customer who has done accurate math and one who is estimating. James used 'probably' twice — that is not settled analysis, that is a guess. The rep had an opening to create doubt about his estimate without fabricating numbers.

quote: It's, seasonal. You know? So, but it's it's relatively small. It probably averages a little less than 100 a month probably for year round.
stewart_read: James uses 'probably' twice in the same sentence when estimating his own bill. This is not a confident self-disqualification — it is an estimate with visible uncertainty. The rep captured the number and moved immediately to a generic pitch. The bill was not flipped. The double 'probably' was not used as a door. A customer who is uncertain about his own bill is a customer who has not actually done the math the rep could do for him.
coaching_implication: In the 1-on-1, highlight the two 'probably's. Ask the rep: 'What does it mean when a customer estimates his own bill with that much uncertainty?' The coaching point is that uncertainty is an opening, not a confirmation. The flip does not require a number: 'Whatever you're paying now goes up every year — the question is whether we can beat it.' That is the move the rep skipped.

quote: Yeah. Of course. Just, you know, just for my notes here, is your electric bill, is it over a $100 a month or is it less?
stewart_read: Framing the bill question as 'just for my notes' is a rapport-preservation move — the rep is trying not to seem pushy after James has declined. The problem is that the framing signals the rep has already accepted the outcome and is just doing paperwork. It removes any sense that the answer matters to James. A bill question framed as data collection does not create curiosity; it confirms the call is over.
alternative_hypothesis: The rep was using a soft frame to keep James talking — a deliberate low-pressure approach to extend the conversation.
coaching_implication: The soft frame is not wrong in principle, but it needs to connect to something James cares about. In the 1-on-1, ask the rep to contrast 'just for my notes' with a frame that makes the answer matter to James: 'The reason I ask is that the payback period you mentioned — that number changes a lot depending on where your bill actually lands.' That keeps the door open without pressure.

quote: Gotcha. Well, I know that our program, it just replaces your current electric bill with a a cheaper solar payment. Right?
stewart_read: This is the rep's only pitch, and it is a generic feature statement that does not reference anything James said. James told the rep he was interested in subsidies, concerned about payback period, and estimated his usage as low. None of those three things appear in this pitch. The 'Right?' tag at the end invites agreement on a claim James has already implicitly rejected. The pitch lands as noise and James exits immediately.
coaching_implication: In the 1-on-1, replay this moment and ask the rep to rebuild the pitch using only James's own words. The coaching target is specificity: 'You mentioned the payback period — that's exactly what the design appointment calculates. Most people who estimate it themselves are off because they don't have the incentive numbers. That's what the specialist shows you.' This is not a harder close — it is a more relevant one.

quote: I, thought you were a solar panel provider, but it sounds like you install complete roofs and solar panels.
stewart_read: James's product-scope confusion came from his prior call, not from this rep. But the rep did not address it — she moved to a clarifying question that confirmed the confusion rather than reframing Ion's value. The confusion ('I thought you were a panel supplier') is actually a door: James wanted to understand solar panels, and Ion's full-service model means he gets more than a supplier would give him. The rep did not use this.
alternative_hypothesis: The rep may have judged that addressing the product-scope confusion would extend a call James was already trying to end.
coaching_implication: In the 1-on-1, ask the rep what she heard when James said 'I thought you were a solar panel provider.' The coaching point is that this confusion is a curiosity hook, not a barrier: 'Actually, because we do the full install, we can show you exactly what the panels would cost on your specific roof — that's the design the specialist puts together.' The prior rep apparently left James with a product-scope misunderstanding; this rep had a chance to correct it and didn't.

quote: my roof is too new to require a new roof, so I was interested in just seeing what the cost of solar panels might be.
stewart_read: James volunteers that his roof is new — which is a positive qualifier, not a barrier. A new roof is one of the best conditions for a solar install. James is framing it as a reason he doesn't need Ion's roofing service, but the rep could have flipped it: a new roof means no re-roof cost, which improves the payback period James is worried about. The rep did not use this.
coaching_implication: In the 1-on-1, highlight that James gave the rep a positive qualifier and the rep did not recognize it as one. The coaching move: 'A new roof is actually ideal for solar — it means the install cost is lower and the payback period you're worried about gets shorter. That's exactly what the design would show you.' This directly addresses James's stated concern using his own information.
